*Auspicio Regis et Senatus Angliae* [Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin") for "By command of the King and Parliament of England" | |

(**EIC**)[[a]](#cite_note-4) was an [English](/wiki/Kingdom_of_England "Kingdom of England"), and later [British](/wiki/British_Empire "British Empire"), [joint-stock company](/wiki/Joint-stock_company "Joint-stock company") that was founded in 1600 and dissolved in 1874.[[4]](#cite_note-Times-5) It was formed to [trade in the Indian Ocean region](/wiki/Indian_Ocean_trade "Indian Ocean trade"), initially with the [East Indies](/wiki/East_Indies "East Indies") (which included the [Indian subcontinent](/wiki/Indian_subcontinent "Indian subcontinent") and Southeast Asia), and later with East Asia. The company gained [control of large parts of the Indian subcontinent](/wiki/Company_rule_in_India "Company rule in India") and [Hong Kong](/wiki/British_Hong_Kong "British Hong Kong"). At its peak, the company was the largest corporation in the world by various measures and had its own armed forces in the form of the company's three [presidency armies](/wiki/Presidency_armies "Presidency armies"), totalling about 260,000 soldiers, twice the size of the [British Army](/wiki/British_Army "British Army") at certain times.[[5]](#cite_note-6)

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

(Redirected from [British East India Company](/w/index.php?title=British_East_India_Company&redirect=no "British East India Company"))

British joint-stock company (1600–1858)

This article is about the British company. For the Dutch company, see [Dutch East India Company](/wiki/Dutch_East_India_Company "Dutch East India Company"). For other uses, see [East India Company (disambiguation)](/wiki/East_India_Company_(disambiguation) "East India Company (disambiguation)").

"John Company" redirects here. For the board game, see [*John Company* (board game)](/wiki/John_Company_(board_game) "John Company (board game)").

| Products | Cotton, silk, [indigo dye](/wiki/Indigo_dye "Indigo dye"), sugar, salt, [spices](/wiki/Spices "Spices"), [slaves](/wiki/Slaves "Slaves"), [saltpetre](/wiki/Potassium_nitrate "Potassium nitrate"), tea, and [opium](/wiki/Opium "Opium") |
| Number of employees | 50,000 (1710s) |

The **East India Company** (**EIC**)[[a]](#cite_note-4) was an [English](/wiki/Kingdom_of_England "Kingdom of England"), and later [British](/wiki/British_Empire "British Empire"), [joint-stock company](/wiki/Joint-stock_company "Joint-stock company") that was founded in 1600 and dissolved in 1874.[[4]](#cite_note-Times-5) It was formed to [trade in the Indian Ocean region](/wiki/Indian_Ocean_trade "Indian Ocean trade"), initially with the [East Indies](/wiki/East_Indies "East Indies") (which included the [Indian subcontinent](/wiki/Indian_subcontinent "Indian subcontinent") and Southeast Asia), and later with East Asia. The company gained [control of large parts of the Indian subcontinent](/wiki/Company_rule_in_India "Company rule in India") and [Hong Kong](/wiki/British_Hong_Kong "British Hong Kong"). At its peak, the company was the largest corporation in the world by various measures and had its own armed forces in the form of the company's three [presidency armies](/wiki/Presidency_armies "Presidency armies"), totalling about 260,000 soldiers, twice the size of the [British Army](/wiki/British_Army "British Army") at certain times.[[5]](#cite_note-6)

Originally [chartered](/wiki/Chartered_company "Chartered company") as the "Governor and Company of Merchants of London Trading into the East-Indies,"[[6]](#cite_note-jiscuk-7)[[7]](#cite_note-8) the company rose to account for half of the world's trade during the mid-1700s and early 1800s,[[8]](#cite_note-onlinegallery-9) particularly in basic commodities including cotton, silk, [indigo dye](/wiki/Indigo_dye "Indigo dye"), sugar, salt, [spices](/wiki/Spices "Spices"), [saltpetre](/wiki/Potassium_nitrate "Potassium nitrate"), tea, [gemstones](/wiki/Gemstone "Gemstone"), and later [opium](/wiki/Opium "Opium"). The company also initiated the beginnings of the [British Raj](/wiki/British_Raj "British Raj") in the Indian subcontinent.[[8]](#cite_note-onlinegallery-9)[[9]](#cite_note-10)

The company eventually came to rule large areas of the Indian subcontinent, exercising [military](/wiki/Presidency_armies "Presidency armies") power and assuming administrative functions. Company-ruled areas in the region gradually expanded after the [Battle of Plassey](/wiki/Battle_of_Plassey "Battle of Plassey") in 1757 and by 1858 most of modern India, Pakistan and Bangladesh, along with [Lower Burma](/wiki/Lower_Burma "Lower Burma"), was either ruled by the company or [princely states](/wiki/Princely_state "Princely state") closely tied to the company by treaties. Following the [Indian Rebellion of 1857](/wiki/Indian_Rebellion_of_1857 "Indian Rebellion of 1857"), the [Government of India Act 1858](/wiki/Government_of_India_Act_1858 "Government of India Act 1858") led to the [British Crown](/wiki/The_Crown "The Crown") assuming direct control of present-day [Bangladesh](/wiki/Bangladesh "Bangladesh"), [India](/wiki/India "India"), [Pakistan](/wiki/Pakistan "Pakistan"), and [Myanmar](/wiki/Myanmar "Myanmar") in the form of the new [British Raj](/wiki/British_Indian_Empire "British Indian Empire").[[10]](#cite_note-Conquests-11)

The company subsequently experienced recurring problems with its finances, despite frequent government intervention. The company was dissolved in 1874 under the terms of the [East India Stock Dividend Redemption Act](/wiki/East_India_Stock_Dividend_Redemption_Act_1873 "East India Stock Dividend Redemption Act 1873") enacted one year earlier, as the Government of India Act had by then rendered it vestigial, powerless, and obsolete. The official government machinery of the British Empire had assumed its governmental functions and absorbed its armies.

[James Lancaster](/wiki/James_Lancaster "James Lancaster") commanded the first East India Company voyage in 1601

In 1577, [Francis Drake](/wiki/Francis_Drake "Francis Drake") set out on [an expedition](/wiki/Francis_Drake%27s_circumnavigation "Francis Drake's circumnavigation") from England to plunder Spanish settlements in South America in search of gold and silver. Sailing in the *[Golden Hind](/wiki/Golden_Hind "Golden Hind")* he achieved this, and then sailed across the Pacific Ocean in 1579, known then only to the Spanish and Portuguese. Drake eventually sailed into the [East Indies](/wiki/East_Indies "East Indies") and came across the [Moluccas](/wiki/Maluku_Islands "Maluku Islands"), also known as the Spice Islands, and met [Sultan Babullah](/wiki/Babullah_of_Ternate "Babullah of Ternate"). In exchange for linen, gold, and silver, the English obtained a large haul of exotic spices, including cloves and nutmeg. Drake returned to England in 1580 and became a hero; his circumnavigation raised an enormous amount of money for England's coffers, and investors received a return of some 5,000 percent. Thus started an important element in the eastern design during the late sixteenth century.[[11]](#cite_note-Lawson2-12)

With England at war with Spain and Portugal in 1585 trade was now cut off, and Queen [Elizabeth I](/wiki/Elizabeth_I "Elizabeth I") resorted to [privateering](/wiki/Privateering "Privateering").[[12]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKeay19939–11-13) Soon after [Thomas Cavendish's circumnavigation](/wiki/Thomas_Cavendish%27s_circumnavigation "Thomas Cavendish's circumnavigation") in 1587 and the [Spanish Armada](/wiki/Spanish_Armada "Spanish Armada")'s defeat the following year, the captured Spanish and Portuguese ships and cargoes laid the foundation for further privateering to even more distant zones of the conflict.[[13]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMilton199941-42-14)[[14]](#cite_note-15) London merchants presented a petition to Elizabeth I for permission to sail to the Indian Ocean, and beyond with the Spice Islands of particular interest.[[15]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMilton199941–42-16)[[16]](#cite_note-igi-ii-p454-17) The aim was to deliver a decisive blow to the Spanish and Portuguese monopoly of far-eastern trade.[[17]](#cite_note-Wernham-18) Elizabeth granted her permission and in 1591, [James Lancaster](/wiki/James_Lancaster "James Lancaster") in the [*Bonaventure*](/wiki/English_ship_Bonaventure_(1567) "English ship Bonaventure (1567)") with two other ships,[[18]](#cite_note-Holmes-19) financed by the [Levant Company](/wiki/Levant_Company "Levant Company"), sailed from England around the [Cape of Good Hope](/wiki/Cape_of_Good_Hope "Cape of Good Hope") to the [Arabian Sea](/wiki/Arabian_Sea "Arabian Sea"), becoming the first English expedition to reach India that way.[[18]](#cite_note-Holmes-19)[[19]](#cite_note-Anarchy-20): 5 Having sailed around [Cape Comorin](/wiki/Cape_Comorin "Cape Comorin") to the [Malay Peninsula](/wiki/Malay_Peninsula "Malay Peninsula"), they preyed on Spanish and Portuguese ships there before returning to England in 1594.[[16]](#cite_note-igi-ii-p454-17)

The biggest prize that galvanised English trade was the seizure of a large Portuguese [carrack](/wiki/Carrack "Carrack"), the *[Madre de Deus](/wiki/Madre_de_Deus "Madre de Deus")*, by [Walter Raleigh](/wiki/Walter_Raleigh "Walter Raleigh") and the [Earl of Cumberland](/wiki/George_Clifford,_3rd_Earl_of_Cumberland "George Clifford, 3rd Earl of Cumberland") at the [Battle of Flores](/wiki/Battle_of_Flores_(1592) "Battle of Flores (1592)") on 13 August 1592.[[20]](#cite_note-McCulloch-21) When she was brought in to [Dartmouth](/wiki/Dartmouth,_Devon "Dartmouth, Devon") she was the largest vessel ever seen in England and she carried chests of jewels, pearls, gold, silver coins, [ambergris](/wiki/Ambergris "Ambergris"), cloth, tapestries, pepper, cloves, cinnamon, nutmeg, [benjamin](/wiki/Benzoin_(resin) "Benzoin (resin)") (a highly aromatic balsamic resin used for perfumes and medicines), red dye, [cochineal](/wiki/Cochineal "Cochineal") and ebony.[[21]](#cite_note-22) Equally valuable was the ship's [rutter](/wiki/Rutter_(nautical) "Rutter (nautical)") (mariner's handbook) containing vital information on the [China](/wiki/Old_China_Trade "Old China Trade"), India, and Japan trade routes.[[20]](#cite_note-McCulloch-21)

In 1596, three more English ships sailed east but all were lost at sea.[[16]](#cite_note-igi-ii-p454-17) A year later however saw the arrival of [Ralph Fitch](/wiki/Ralph_Fitch "Ralph Fitch"), an adventurer merchant who, with his companions, had made a remarkable nine year overland journey to [Mesopotamia](/wiki/Mesopotamia "Mesopotamia"), the [Persian Gulf](/wiki/Persian_Gulf "Persian Gulf"), the Indian Ocean, India and Southeast Asia.[[22]](#cite_note-23) Fitch was consulted on Indian affairs and gave even more valuable information to Lancaster.[[23]](#cite_note-24)

### Formation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=East_India_Company&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Formation")]

In 1599, a group of prominent merchants and explorers met to discuss a potential East Indies venture under a [royal charter](/wiki/Royal_charter "Royal charter").[[19]](#cite_note-Anarchy-20): 1–2 Besides Fitch[[19]](#cite_note-Anarchy-20): 5 the group included [Stephen Soame](/wiki/Stephen_Soame "Stephen Soame"), then [Lord Mayor of London](/wiki/Lord_Mayor_of_London "Lord Mayor of London"); [Thomas Smythe](/wiki/Thomas_Smythe "Thomas Smythe"), a powerful London politician and administrator; [Richard Hakluyt](/wiki/Richard_Hakluyt "Richard Hakluyt"), writer and proponent of [English colonisation of the Americas](/wiki/British_colonization_of_the_Americas "British colonization of the Americas"); and several other sea-farers who had served with Drake and Raleigh.[[19]](#cite_note-Anarchy-20): 1–2

On 22 September, the group stated their intention "to venture in the pretended voyage to the East Indies (the which it may please the Lord to prosper)" and to themselves invest £30,133 (over £4,000,000 in today's money).[[24]](#cite_note-25)[[25]](#cite_note-british-history.ac.uk-26) Two days later, the "Adventurers" reconvened and resolved to apply to the Queen for support of the project.[[25]](#cite_note-british-history.ac.uk-26) Although their first attempt had not been completely successful, they sought the Queen's unofficial approval to continue. They bought ships for the venture and increased their investment to £68,373.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

They convened again a year later, on 31 December 1600, and this time they succeeded; the Queen responded favourably to a petition by [George, Earl of Cumberland](/wiki/George_Clifford,_3rd_Earl_of_Cumberland "George Clifford, 3rd Earl of Cumberland") and 218 others,[[26]](#cite_note-27) including [James Lancaster](/wiki/James_Lancaster "James Lancaster"), [Sir John Harte](/wiki/John_Harte_(mayor) "John Harte (mayor)"), [Sir John Spencer](/wiki/John_Spencer_(Lord_Mayor_of_London) "John Spencer (Lord Mayor of London)") (both of whom had been [Lord Mayor of London](/wiki/Lord_Mayor_of_London "Lord Mayor of London")), the adventurer [Edward Michelborne](/wiki/Edward_Michelborne "Edward Michelborne"), the nobleman [William Cavendish](/wiki/William_Cavendish,_1st_Earl_of_Devonshire "William Cavendish, 1st Earl of Devonshire") and other [aldermen](/wiki/Aldermen "Aldermen") and citizens.[[27]](#cite_note-EIC-Charters-28) She granted her charter to their corporation named **Governor and Company of Merchants of London trading into the East Indies**.[[16]](#cite_note-igi-ii-p454-17) For a period of fifteen years, the charter awarded the company a monopoly[[28]](#cite_note-29) on English trade with all countries east of the Cape of Good Hope and west of the [Straits of Magellan](/wiki/Straits_of_Magellan "Straits of Magellan").[[29]](#cite_note-30) Any traders there without a licence from the company were liable to forfeiture of their ships and cargo (half of which would go to the Crown and half to the company), as well as imprisonment at the "royal pleasure".[[30]](#cite_note-31)

The charter named [Thomas Smythe](/wiki/Thomas_Smythe "Thomas Smythe") as the first governor[[27]](#cite_note-EIC-Charters-28): 3 of the company, and 24 [directors](/wiki/British_East_India_Company_directors "British East India Company directors") (including James Lancaster)[[27]](#cite_note-EIC-Charters-28): 4 or "committees", who made up a Court of Directors. They, in turn, reported to a Court of Proprietors, who appointed them. Ten committees reported to the Court of Directors. By tradition, business was initially transacted at the Nags Head Inn, opposite [St Botolph's](/wiki/St_Botolph-without-Bishopsgate "St Botolph-without-Bishopsgate") church in [Bishopsgate](/wiki/Bishopsgate "Bishopsgate"), before moving to East India House on [Leadenhall Street](/wiki/Leadenhall_Street "Leadenhall Street").[[31]](#cite_note-32)

### Early voyages to the East Indies

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=East_India_Company&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Early voyages to the East Indies")]

[Sir James Lancaster](/wiki/Sir_James_Lancaster "Sir James Lancaster") commanded the first East India Company voyage in 1601 aboard [*Red Dragon*](/wiki/Red_Dragon_(ship) "Red Dragon (ship)").[[32]](#cite_note-33) The following year, whilst sailing in the [Malacca Straits](/wiki/Malacca_Strait "Malacca Strait"), Lancaster took the rich 1,200 ton Portuguese carrack *Sao Thome* carrying pepper and spices. The booty enabled the voyagers to set up two "[factories](/wiki/Factory_(trading_post) "Factory (trading post)")" (trading posts) – one at [Bantam](/wiki/Bantam_(city) "Bantam (city)") on [Java](/wiki/Java "Java") and another in the [Moluccas](/wiki/Molucca "Molucca") (Spice Islands) before leaving.[[33]](#cite_note-Dulles106-34) On return to England in 1603, they learned of Elizabeth's death, but Lancaster was knighted by the new king, [James I](/wiki/James_VI_and_I "James VI and I"), on account of the voyage's success.[[34]](#cite_note-35) By this time, the [war with Spain](/wiki/Anglo-Spanish_War_(1585%E2%80%931604) "Anglo-Spanish War (1585–1604)") had ended but the company had profitably breached the Spanish-Portuguese duopoly; new horizons opened for the English.[[17]](#cite_note-Wernham-18)

In March 1604, Sir [Henry Middleton](/wiki/Henry_Middleton_(captain) "Henry Middleton (captain)") commanded the company's [second voyage](/wiki/Second_voyage_to_Asia "Second voyage to Asia"). General [William Keeling](/wiki/William_Keeling "William Keeling"), a captain during the second voyage, led the third voyage aboard *Red Dragon* from 1607 to 1610 along with *Hector* under Captain [William Hawkins](/wiki/Sir_William_Hawkins "Sir William Hawkins") and *Consent* under Captain [David Middleton](/wiki/David_Middleton_(mariner) "David Middleton (mariner)").[[35]](#cite_note-East_India_Company_1897_vi-36)

Early in 1608, [Alexander Sharpeigh](/wiki/Alexander_Sharpeigh "Alexander Sharpeigh") was made captain of the company's *Ascension*, and general or commander of the fourth voyage. Thereafter two ships, *Ascension* and *Union* (captained by Richard Rowles), sailed from Woolwich on 14 March 1608.[[35]](#cite_note-East_India_Company_1897_vi-36) This expedition was lost.[[36]](#cite_note-Mill1817-37)

Initially, the company struggled in the [spice trade](/wiki/Spice_trade "Spice trade") because of competition from the [Dutch East India Company](/wiki/Dutch_East_India_Company "Dutch East India Company"). This rivalry led to military skirmishes, with each company establishing fortified trading posts, fleets, and alliances with local rulers. The Dutch, better financed and supported by their government, gained the upper hand by establishing a stronghold in the spice islands (now [Indonesia](/wiki/Indonesia "Indonesia")), enforcing a near-monopoly through aggressive policies that eventually drove the EIC to seek trade opportunities in India instead. The English company opened a [factory](/wiki/Factory_(trading_post) "Factory (trading post)") (trading post) in [Bantam](/wiki/Banten_(town) "Banten (town)") on Java on its first voyage, and imports of [pepper](/wiki/Black_pepper "Black pepper") from Java remained an important part of the company's trade for twenty years.[[37]](#cite_note-38)

The emperor [Jahangir](/wiki/Jahangir "Jahangir") investing a courtier with a robe of honour, watched by [Sir Thomas Roe](/wiki/Sir_Thomas_Roe "Sir Thomas Roe"), English ambassador to the court of Jahangir at Agra from 1615 to 1618, and others

English traders frequently fought their Dutch and Portuguese counterparts in the Indian Ocean. The company achieved a major victory over the Portuguese in the [Battle of Swally](/wiki/Battle_of_Swally "Battle of Swally") in 1612, at [Suvali](/wiki/Suvali "Suvali") in [Surat](/wiki/Surat "Surat"). The company decided to explore the feasibility of a foothold in mainland India, with official sanction from both Britain and the [Mughal Empire](/wiki/Mughal_Empire "Mughal Empire"), and requested that the Crown launch a diplomatic mission.[[38]](#cite_note-fordham1-39)

#### Foothold in India

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=East_India_Company&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Foothold in India")]

See also: [Establishment of English trade in Bengal (1610–1900)](/wiki/History_of_Kolkata#Establishment_of_English_trade_in_Bengal_(1610–1900) "History of Kolkata")

Company ships docked at [Surat](/wiki/Surat "Surat") in [Gujarat](/wiki/Gujarat_under_Mughal_Empire "Gujarat under Mughal Empire") in 1608.[[39]](#cite_note-Tracy-40) The company's first Indian factory was established in 1611 at [Masulipatnam](/wiki/Machilipatnam "Machilipatnam") on the [Andhra Coast](/wiki/Coastal_Andhra "Coastal Andhra") of the [Bay of Bengal](/wiki/Bay_of_Bengal "Bay of Bengal"), and its second in 1615 at Surat.[[40]](#cite_note-41)[[39]](#cite_note-Tracy-40) The high profits reported by the company after landing in India initially prompted James I to grant subsidiary licences to other trading companies in England. However, in 1609, he renewed the East India Company's charter for an indefinite period, with the proviso that its privileges would be annulled if trade was unprofitable for three consecutive years.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In 1615, James I instructed Sir [Thomas Roe](/wiki/Thomas_Roe "Thomas Roe") to visit the Mughal Emperor Nur-ud-din Salim [Jahangir](/wiki/Jahangir "Jahangir") (r. 1605–1627) to arrange for a commercial treaty that would give the company exclusive rights to reside and establish factories in Surat and other areas. In return, the company offered to provide the Emperor with goods and rarities from the European market. This mission was highly successful, and Jahangir sent a letter to James through Sir Thomas Roe:[[38]](#cite_note-fordham1-39)

> Upon which assurance of your royal love I have given my general command to all the kingdoms and ports of my dominions to receive all the merchants of the English nation as the subjects of my friend; that in what place soever they choose to live, they may have free liberty without any restraint; and at what port soever they shall arrive, that neither Portugal nor any other shall dare to molest their quiet; and in what city soever they shall have residence, I have commanded all my governors and captains to give them freedom answerable to their own desires; to sell, buy, and to transport into their country at their pleasure.
> For confirmation of our love and friendship, I desire your Majesty to command your merchants to bring in their ships of all sorts of rarities and rich goods fit for my palace; and that you be pleased to send me your royal letters by every opportunity, that I may rejoice in your health and prosperous affairs; that our friendship may be interchanged and eternal.

— Nuruddin Salim Jahangir, Letter to James I.

#### Expansion in present day South Asia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=East_India_Company&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Expansion in present day South Asia")]

See also: [List of Anglo-Indian wars](/wiki/List_of_Anglo-Indian_wars "List of Anglo-Indian wars")

The company, which benefited from the imperial patronage, soon expanded its commercial trading operations. It eclipsed the Portuguese [Estado da Índia](/wiki/Estado_da_%C3%8Dndia "Estado da Índia"), which had established bases in [Goa](/wiki/Goa "Goa"), [Chittagong](/wiki/Chittagong "Chittagong"), and [Bombay](/wiki/Bombay "Bombay"); Portugal later ceded Bombay to England as part of the [dowry](/wiki/Dowry "Dowry") of [Catherine of Braganza](/wiki/Catherine_of_Braganza "Catherine of Braganza") on her marriage to King [Charles II](/wiki/Charles_II_of_England "Charles II of England"). The East India Company also launched a joint attack with the Dutch [United East India Company](/wiki/United_East_India_Company "United East India Company") (VOC) on Portuguese and Spanish ships off the coast of China that helped secure EIC ports in China,[[41]](#cite_note-42) independently attacking the Portuguese in the [Persian Gulf Residencies](/wiki/Persian_Gulf_Residency "Persian Gulf Residency") primarily for political reasons.[[42]](#cite_note-43) The company established [trading posts](/wiki/Trading_post "Trading post") in [Surat](/wiki/Surat "Surat") (1619) and [Madras](/wiki/Chennai "Chennai") (1639).[[43]](#cite_note-Cadell1956-44) By 1647, the company had 23 factories and settlements in India, and 90 employees.[[44]](#cite_note-45) Many of the major factories became some of the most populated and commercially influential cities in Bengal, including the walled forts of [Fort William](/wiki/Fort_William,_India "Fort William, India") in Bengal, [Fort St George](/wiki/Fort_St_George "Fort St George") in Madras, and [Bombay Castle](/wiki/Bombay_Castle "Bombay Castle").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The first century of the Company, despite its original profits coming primarily from piracy in the [Spice Islands](/wiki/Maluku_Islands "Maluku Islands") between competing European powers and their companies,[[45]](#cite_note-Dalrymple2019-46) saw the East India Company change focus after suffering a major setback in 1623 when their factory in [Amboyna](/wiki/Amboyna_massacre "Amboyna massacre") in the Moluccas was attacked by the Dutch. This compelled the company to formally abandon their efforts in the Spice Islands, and turn their attention to Bengal where, by this time, they were making steady, if less exciting, profits.[[45]](#cite_note-Dalrymple2019-46) After gaining the indifferent patronage of the [Mughal Empire](/wiki/Mughal_Empire "Mughal Empire"), whose cities were 'the megacities of their time' and whose wealth was unrivaled outside of Asia in the 17th century,[[45]](#cite_note-Dalrymple2019-46) the Company's first century in the Mughal-ruled areas was spent cultivating their relationship with the [Mughal Dynasty](/wiki/Mughal_dynasty "Mughal dynasty"), and conducting peaceful trade at great profit. At first it should be said the EIC was drawn into the Mughal system, acting as a kind of vassal to Mughal authority in present-day Bangladesh: it was from this position that the Company would ultimately outplay and outmanoeuvre all competing powers in the region, to eventually use that very system to hold power itself.[[45]](#cite_note-Dalrymple2019-46)

What started as trading posts on undesirable land were developed into sprawling factory complexes with hundreds of workers sending exotic goods to England and managing protected points to export English finished goods to local merchants. The Company's initial rise in Bengal and successes generally came at the expense of competing European powers through the art of currying favours and well-placed bribes, as the Company was matched at every step with French expansion in the region (whose [equivalent company](/wiki/Louis_XIV%27s_East_India_Company "Louis XIV's East India Company") carried substantial royal support). Throughout the entire century the company only resorted to force against the Mughals once, with terrible consequences.[[45]](#cite_note-Dalrymple2019-46) The [Anglo-Mughal war (1686–1690)](/wiki/Anglo-Mughal_war_(1686%E2%80%931690) "Anglo-Mughal war (1686–1690)") was a complete defeat, ending when the EIC effectively swore fealty to the Mughals to get their factories back.

The East India Company's fortunes changed for the better in 1707 when Bengal and other regions under Mughal rule fell into anarchy after the death of the Mughal Emperor [Aurangzeb](/wiki/Aurangzeb "Aurangzeb").[[45]](#cite_note-Dalrymple2019-46) A series of large-scale rebellions, and the collapse of the Mughal taxation system led to the effective independence of virtually all of the pre-1707 Mughal fiefs and holdings, with their capital Delhi routinely under the control of Maratha, Afghan, or usurper generals' armies. The EIC was able to take advantage of this chaos, slowly assuming direct control of the province of [Bengal](/wiki/Bengal "Bengal"), and fighting [numerous wars against the French](/wiki/Carnatic_wars "Carnatic wars") for control of the east coast of the subcontinent. The Company's position in the Mughal court as it fell apart made it possible to sponsor various powerful people on the subcontinent as they individually contended with others, steadily amassing more land and power in India to themselves.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In the 18th Century, the primary source of the Company's profits in Bengal became taxation in conquered and controlled provinces, as the factories became fortresses and administrative hubs for networks of tax collectors that expanded into enormous cities. The Mughal Empire was the richest in the world in 1700, and the East India Company tried to strip it bare for a century thereafter. Dalrymple calls it "the single largest transfer of wealth until the Nazis."[[45]](#cite_note-Dalrymple2019-46) What was in the 17th century the production capital of the world for textiles was forced to become a market for British-made textiles. Statues, jewels, and various other valuables were moved from the palaces of Bengal to the townhouses of the English countryside. Bengal in particular suffered the worst of Company tax farming, highlighted by the [Great Bengal famine of 1770](/wiki/Great_Bengal_famine_of_1770 "Great Bengal famine of 1770").[[45]](#cite_note-Dalrymple2019-46)

The primary tool of expansion for the company was the Sepoy. The [Sepoys](/wiki/Sepoy "Sepoy") were locally raised with European training and equipment, who changed warfare in present-day South Asia. Mounted forces and their superior mobility had been king on the region's battlefields for a thousand years, with cannon so well integrated that the Mughals fought with cannon mounted on elephants; all were no match to line infantry with decent discipline supported with field cannon. Repeatedly, a few thousand company sepoys fought vastly larger Mughal forces numerically and came out victorious. Afghan, Mughal and Maratha factions started creating their own European-style forces, often with French equipment, as the chaos intensified and the stakes were raised. Ultimately, the company won out, generally through as much diplomacy and state-craft as through fraud and deception.

The gradual rise of the East India Company within the Mughal network culminated in the [Second Anglo-Maratha War](/wiki/Second_Anglo-Maratha_War "Second Anglo-Maratha War"), in which the Company successfully ousted the Maratha, the Empire's official protectors, at the high water point in their rise to power, and installed a young Mughal Prince as Emperor, with the Company as the de jure protectors of the Empire from their position of direct control in Bengal. This relationship was repeatedly strained as the Company continued its expansion and exploitation, however it lasted in some form until 1858, when the last Mughal Emperor was exiled as the Company was disbanded and its assets were taken over by the British Crown.[[45]](#cite_note-Dalrymple2019-46)

In 1634, the Mughal emperor [Shah Jahan](/wiki/Shah_Jahan "Shah Jahan") extended his hospitality to English traders to [Bengal](/wiki/Bengal "Bengal"), the richest region of the empire,[[46]](#cite_note-47) and in 1717 customs duties were completely waived for the English in Bengal. By then, the Company's mainstay businesses were in cotton, silk, opium, [indigo dye](/wiki/Indigo_dye "Indigo dye"), [saltpetre](/wiki/Potassium_nitrate "Potassium nitrate") and tea. Meanwhile, the Dutch, the Company's most aggressive competitors, had expanded their monopoly of the spice trade in the [Straits of Malacca](/wiki/Straits_of_Malacca "Straits of Malacca") by ousting the Portuguese in 1640–1641. With reduced Portuguese and Spanish influence in the region, the EIC and VOC entered a period of intense competition, resulting in the [Anglo-Dutch wars](/wiki/Anglo-Dutch_wars "Anglo-Dutch wars") of the 17th and 18th centuries. The British were also interested in trans-Himalayan trade routes, as they would create access to untapped markets for British manufactured goods in Tibet and China.[[47]](#cite_note-48) This economic interest was showcased by the [Anglo-Nepalese war (1814–1816).](/wiki/Anglo-Nepalese_war "Anglo-Nepalese war")

#### Expansion throughout Asia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=East_India_Company&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Expansion throughout Asia")]

Within the first two decades of the 17th century, the Dutch East India Company or *Vereenigde Oostindische Compagnie*, (VOC) was the wealthiest commercial operation in the world with 50,000 employees worldwide and a private fleet of 200 ships. It specialised in the spice trade and gave its shareholders 40% annual dividend.[[48]](#cite_note-49)[*[better source needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability#Questionable_sources "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*]

The British East India Company was fiercely competitive with the Dutch and French throughout the 17th and 18th centuries over spices from the [Spice Islands](/wiki/Spice_Islands "Spice Islands"). Some spices, at the time, could only be found on these islands, such as nutmeg and cloves; and they could bring profits as high as 400 per cent from one voyage.[[49]](#cite_note-Suijk2015-50)

The tension was so high between the Dutch and the British East Indies Trading Companies that it escalated into at least four Anglo-Dutch wars:[[49]](#cite_note-Suijk2015-50) 1652–1654, 1665–1667, 1672–1674 and 1780–1784.

Competition arose in 1635 when Charles I granted a trading licence to Sir [William Courteen](/wiki/William_Courten "William Courten"), which permitted the rival [Courteen association](/wiki/Courteen_association "Courteen association") to trade with the east at any location in which the EIC had no presence.[[50]](#cite_note-51)

In an act aimed at strengthening the power of the EIC, King Charles II granted the EIC (in a series of five acts around 1670) the rights to autonomous territorial acquisitions, to mint money, to command fortresses and troops and form alliances, to make war and peace, and to exercise both civil and criminal jurisdiction over the acquired areas.[[51]](#cite_note-52)

In 1689, a Mughal fleet commanded by [Sidi Yaqub](/wiki/Sidi_Yaqub "Sidi Yaqub") attacked Bombay. After a year of resistance the EIC surrendered in 1690, and the company sent envoys to [Aurangzeb](/wiki/Aurangzeb "Aurangzeb")'s camp to plead for a pardon. The company's envoys had to prostrate themselves before the emperor, pay a large indemnity, and promise better behaviour in the future. The emperor withdrew his troops, and the company subsequently re-established itself in Bombay and set up a new base in Calcutta.[[52]](#cite_note-encyclopedia.com-53)

#### Slavery 1621–1834

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=East_India_Company&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Slavery 1621–1834")]

The East India Company's archives suggest its involvement in the slave trade began in 1684, when a Captain Robert Knox was ordered to buy and transport 250 slaves from [Madagascar](/wiki/Madagascar "Madagascar") to [St. Helena](/wiki/St._Helena "St. Helena").[[54]](#cite_note-55) The East India Company began using and transporting slaves in Asia and the Atlantic in the early 1620s, according to the [Encyclopædia Britannica](/wiki/Encyclop%C3%A6dia_Britannica "Encyclopædia Britannica"),[[1]](#cite_note-topic/East-1) or in 1621, according to Richard Allen.[[55]](#cite_note-56) Eventually, the company ended the trade in 1834 after numerous legal threats from the British state and the [Royal Navy](/wiki/Royal_Navy "Royal Navy") in the form of the [West Africa Squadron](/wiki/West_Africa_Squadron "West Africa Squadron"), which discovered various ships had contained evidence of the illegal trade.[[56]](#cite_note-57)

#### Japan

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=East_India_Company&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Japan")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1e/Bodleian_Library_MS._Jap._b.2_Shuinjo.jpg/250px-Bodleian_Library_MS._Jap._b.2_Shuinjo.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bodleian_Library_MS._Jap._b.2_Shuinjo.jpg)

A document with the original [vermilion](/wiki/Vermilion "Vermilion") seal of [Tokugawa Ieyasu](/wiki/Tokugawa_Ieyasu "Tokugawa Ieyasu"), granting trade privileges in Japan to the East India Company in 1613

In 1613, during the rule of [Tokugawa Hidetada](/wiki/Tokugawa_Hidetada "Tokugawa Hidetada") of the [Tokugawa shogunate](/wiki/Tokugawa_shogunate "Tokugawa shogunate"), the British ship [*Clove*](/wiki/Clove_(ship) "Clove (ship)"), under the command of Captain [John Saris](/wiki/John_Saris "John Saris"), was the first English ship to call on Japan. Saris was the chief factor of the EIC's trading post in Java, and with the assistance of [William Adams](/wiki/William_Adams_(sailor,_born_1564) "William Adams (sailor, born 1564)"), an English sailor who had arrived in Japan in 1600, he was able to gain permission from the ruler to establish a commercial house in [Hirado](/wiki/Hirado,_Nagasaki "Hirado, Nagasaki") on the Japanese island of [Kyushu](/wiki/Kyushu "Kyushu"):

> We give free license to the subjects of the King of Great Britaine, Sir Thomas Smythe, Governor and Company of the East Indian Merchants and Adventurers forever safely come into any of our ports of our Empire of Japan with their shippes and merchandise, without any hindrance to them or their goods, and to abide, buy, sell and barter according to their own manner with all nations, to tarry here as long as they think good, and to depart at their pleasure.[[57]](#cite_note-58)

Unable to obtain Japanese [raw silk](/wiki/Raw_silk "Raw silk") for export to China, and with their trading area reduced to Hirado and [Nagasaki](/wiki/Nagasaki "Nagasaki") from 1616 onwards, the company closed its factory in 1623.[[58]](#cite_note-59)

#### Anglo-Mughal war

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=East_India_Company&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Anglo-Mughal war")]

Main article: [Anglo-Mughal war (1686–1690)](/wiki/Anglo-Mughal_war_(1686%E2%80%931690) "Anglo-Mughal war (1686–1690)")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fb/The_English_ask_pardon_of_Aurangzeb.jpg/250px-The_English_ask_pardon_of_Aurangzeb.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_English_ask_pardon_of_Aurangzeb.jpg)

[French](/wiki/Kingdom_of_France "Kingdom of France") illustration of [Sir Josiah Child](/wiki/Josiah_Child "Josiah Child") requesting a pardon from the [Emperor](/wiki/Mughal_Emperor "Mughal Emperor") [Aurangzeb](/wiki/Aurangzeb "Aurangzeb")

The first of the [Anglo-Indian wars](/wiki/Anglo-Indian_wars "Anglo-Indian wars") occurred in 1686 when the company conducted naval operations against [Shaista Khan](/wiki/Shaista_Khan "Shaista Khan"), the governor of [Mughal Bengal](/wiki/Mughal_Bengal "Mughal Bengal"). This led to the siege of Bombay and the subsequent intervention of the Mughal Emperor, [Aurangzeb](/wiki/Aurangzeb "Aurangzeb"). Subsequently, the English company was defeated and fined.[[59]](#cite_note-60)[[60]](#cite_note-61)

**Mughal convoy piracy incident of 1695**

Main article: [Ganj-i-Sawai](/wiki/Ganj-i-Sawai "Ganj-i-Sawai")

In September 1695, Captain [Henry Every](/wiki/Henry_Every "Henry Every"), an English pirate on board the [*Fancy*](/wiki/Fancy#Ships "Fancy"), reached the Straits of [Bab-el-Mandeb](/wiki/Bab-el-Mandeb "Bab-el-Mandeb"),[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] where he teamed up with five other pirate captains to make an attack on the Indian fleet returning from the annual pilgrimage to [Mecca](/wiki/Mecca "Mecca"). The Mughal convoy included the treasure-laden *[Ganj-i-Sawai](/wiki/Ganj-i-Sawai "Ganj-i-Sawai")*, reported to be the greatest in the Mughal fleet and the largest ship operational in the Indian Ocean, and its escort, the *Fateh Muhammed*. They were spotted passing the straits en route to [Surat](/wiki/Surat "Surat"). The pirates gave chase and caught up with the *Fateh Muhammed* some days later, and meeting little resistance, took some £40,000 of silver.[[61]](#cite_note-Burgess_2009-62): 136–137

Every continued in pursuit and managed to overhaul *Ganj-i-Sawai*, which resisted strongly before eventually [striking](/wiki/Striking_the_colours "Striking the colours"). *Ganj-i-Sawai* carried enormous wealth and, according to contemporary East India Company sources, was carrying a relative of the [Grand Mughal](/wiki/Aurangazeb "Aurangazeb"), though there is no evidence to suggest that it was his daughter and her retinue. The loot from the *Ganj-i-Sawai* had a total value between £325,000 and £600,000, including 500,000 gold and silver pieces, and has become known as the richest ship ever taken by pirates.[[62]](#cite_note-63)

When the news arrived in England it caused an outcry. To appease Aurangzeb, the East India Company promised to pay all financial reparations, while [Parliament](/wiki/Parliament_of_the_United_Kingdom "Parliament of the United Kingdom") declared the pirates *[hostis humani generis](/wiki/Hostis_humani_generis "Hostis humani generis")* ("the enemy of humanity").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] In mid-1696 the government issued a £500 bounty on Every's head and offered a free pardon to any informer who disclosed his whereabouts. The first worldwide manhunt in recorded history was underway.[[61]](#cite_note-Burgess_2009-62): 144

The plunder of Aurangzeb's treasure ship had serious consequences for the English East India Company. The furious Mughal Emperor Aurangzeb ordered Sidi Yaqub and [Nawab Daud Khan](/wiki/Nawab_Daud_Khan "Nawab Daud Khan") to attack and close four of the company's factories in India and imprison their officers, who were almost lynched by a mob of angry [Mughals](/wiki/Mughal_(tribe) "Mughal (tribe)"), blaming them for their countryman's depredations, and threatened to put an end to all English trading in India. To appease Emperor Aurangzeb and particularly his [Grand Vizier](/wiki/Grand_Vizier "Grand Vizier") [Asad Khan](/wiki/Asad_Khan_(Mughal_noble) "Asad Khan (Mughal noble)"), Parliament exempted Every from all of the [Acts of Grace](/wiki/Acts_of_grace_(piracy) "Acts of grace (piracy)") (pardons) and amnesties it would subsequently issue to other pirates.[[63]](#cite_note-64)[*[disputed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Disputed_statement "Wikipedia:Disputed statement") – [discuss](/wiki/Talk:East_India_Company#Henry_Avery_and_Acts_of_Grace "Talk:East India Company")*]

#### China

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=East_India_Company&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: China")]

The British began trading with China in 1699.[[64]](#cite_note-65) This fact is recorded from the Chinese side in *[The Draft History of the Qing](/wiki/Qingshi_gao "Qingshi gao")* under the year Kangxi 37 (1698).[[65]](#cite_note-66) The apparent discrepancy between the British and Chinese sources can be explained by the fact that [Chinese New Year](/wiki/Chinese_New_Year "Chinese New Year") of 1699 did not fall until 31 January, so that any treaties entered into in January would have been logged under Kangxi 37 rather than Kangxi 38. In 1715 the Company established a permanent "[factory](/wiki/Factory_(trading_post) "Factory (trading post)")" in [Guangzhou](/wiki/Guangzhou "Guangzhou") (Canton).[[66]](#cite_note-67)

The company started selling opium to Chinese merchants in the 1770s in exchange for goods like [porcelain](/wiki/Porcelain "Porcelain") and tea,[[67]](#cite_note-68) causing a series of [opioid addiction](/wiki/Opioid_use_disorder "Opioid use disorder") outbreaks across China in 1820.[[68]](#cite_note-69) The ruling [Qing dynasty](/wiki/Qing_dynasty "Qing dynasty") outlawed the opium trade in 1796 and 1800,[[69]](#cite_note-70) but British merchants continued illegally nonetheless.[[70]](#cite_note-71)[[71]](#cite_note-72) The Qing took measures to prevent the East India Company from selling opium, and destroyed tens of thousands of chests of opium already in the country.[[72]](#cite_note-73) This series of events led to the [First Opium War](/wiki/First_Opium_War "First Opium War") in 1839, which involved a succession of British naval attacks along the Chinese coast over the course of several months. As part of the [Treaty of Nanjing](/wiki/Treaty_of_Nanking "Treaty of Nanking") in 1842, the Qing were forced to give British merchants special treatment and the right to sell opium. The Chinese also ceded territory to the British, including the island of [Hong Kong](/wiki/Hong_Kong_Island "Hong Kong Island").[[73]](#cite_note-74)

### Forming a complete monopoly

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=East_India_Company&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Forming a complete monopoly")]

#### Trade monopoly

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=East_India_Company&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Trade monopoly")]

The prosperity that the officers of the company enjoyed allowed them to return to Britain and establish sprawling estates and businesses, and to obtain political power, such as seats in the House of Commons.[[74]](#cite_note-Guha2016-75) Ship captains sold their appointments to successors for up to £500. As recruits aimed to return to Britain wealthy by securing Indian money, their loyalties to their homeland increased.[[74]](#cite_note-Guha2016-75)

The company developed a [lobby](/wiki/Lobbying "Lobbying") in the English parliament. Pressure from ambitious tradesmen and former company associates (pejoratively termed *Interlopers* by the company), who wanted to establish private trading firms in India, led to the passing of the deregulating act in 1694.[[75]](#cite_note-victorianweb.org-76)

This act allowed any English firm to trade with India, unless specifically prohibited by act of parliament, thereby annulling the charter that had been in force for almost 100 years. When the East India Company Act 1697 ([9 Will. 3](/wiki/9_Will._3 "9 Will. 3"). c. 44) was passed in 1697, a new "parallel" East India Company (officially titled the *English Company Trading to the East Indies*) was floated under a state-backed indemnity of £2 million.[[76]](#cite_note-Boggart-77) The powerful stockholders of the old company quickly subscribed a sum of £315,000 in the new concern, and dominated the new body. The two companies wrestled with each other for some time, both in England and in India, for a dominant share of the trade.[[75]](#cite_note-victorianweb.org-76)

It quickly became evident that, in practice, the original company faced scarcely any measurable competition. The companies merged in 1708, by a tripartite indenture involving both companies and the state, with the charter and agreement for the new *United Company of Merchants of England Trading to the East Indies* being awarded by [Sidney Godolphin, 1st Earl of Godolphin](/wiki/Sidney_Godolphin,_1st_Earl_of_Godolphin "Sidney Godolphin, 1st Earl of Godolphin").[[77]](#cite_note-78) Under this arrangement, the merged company lent a sum of £3,200,000 to the Treasury, in return for exclusive privileges for the next three years, after which the situation was to be reviewed. The amalgamated company became the *United Company of Merchants of England Trading to the East Indies*.[[75]](#cite_note-victorianweb.org-76)

A constant battle between the company lobby and Parliament followed for decades. The company sought a permanent establishment, while Parliament would not willingly allow it greater autonomy and so relinquish the opportunity to exploit the company's profits. In 1712, another act renewed the status of the company, though the debts were repaid. By 1720, 15% of British imports were from India, almost all passing through the company, which reasserted the influence of the company lobby. The licence was prolonged until 1766 by yet another act in 1730.[[78]](#cite_note-82)

At this time, Britain and France became bitter rivals. Frequent skirmishes between them took place for control of colonial possessions. In 1742, fearing the monetary consequences of a war, the British government agreed to extend the deadline for the licensed exclusive trade by the company in India until 1783, in return for a further loan of £1 million. Between 1756 and 1763, the [Seven Years' War](/wiki/Seven_Years%27_War "Seven Years' War") diverted the state's attention towards consolidation and [defence of its territorial possessions](/wiki/French_and_Indian_War "French and Indian War") in Europe and its [colonies in North America](/wiki/English_colonisation_of_the_Americas "English colonisation of the Americas").[[79]](#cite_note-oxforddnb.com-83)

The war partly took place in the Indian theatre, between the company troops and the French forces. In 1757, the [Law Officers of the Crown](/wiki/Law_Officers_of_the_Crown "Law Officers of the Crown") delivered the [Pratt–Yorke opinion](/wiki/Pratt%E2%80%93Yorke_opinion "Pratt–Yorke opinion") distinguishing overseas territories acquired by [right of conquest](/wiki/Right_of_conquest "Right of conquest") from those acquired by private [treaty](/wiki/Treaty "Treaty"). The opinion asserted that, while the Crown of Great Britain enjoyed sovereignty over both, only the property of the former was vested in the Crown.[[79]](#cite_note-oxforddnb.com-83)

With the advent of the [Industrial Revolution](/wiki/Industrial_Revolution "Industrial Revolution"), Britain surged ahead of its European rivals. Demand for Indian commodities was boosted by the need to sustain troops and the economy during the war, and by the increased availability of raw materials and efficient methods of production. As home to the revolution, Britain experienced higher standards of living. Its ever-growing cycle of prosperity, demand and production had a profound influence on overseas trade. The company became the single largest player in the British global market. In 1801 [Henry Dundas](/wiki/Henry_Dundas,_1st_Viscount_Melville "Henry Dundas, 1st Viscount Melville") reported to the [House of Commons](/wiki/House_of_Commons_of_the_United_Kingdom "House of Commons of the United Kingdom") that

> ... on the 1st March, 1801, the debts of the East India Company amounted to 5,393,989*[l.](/wiki/Pound_sterling "Pound sterling")* their effects to 15,404,736*l.* and that their sales had increased since February 1793, from 4,988,300*l.* to 7,602,041*l.*[[80]](#cite_note-84)

#### Saltpetre trade

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=East_India_Company&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Saltpetre trade")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b3/Potassium_nitrate.jpg/250px-Potassium_nitrate.jpg)](/wiki/File:Potassium_nitrate.jpg)

[Saltpetre](/wiki/Potassium_nitrate "Potassium nitrate") used for [gunpowder](/wiki/Gunpowder "Gunpowder") was one of the major trade goods of the company

Sir [John Banks](/wiki/Sir_John_Banks,_1st_Baronet "Sir John Banks, 1st Baronet"), a businessman from [Kent](/wiki/Kent "Kent") who negotiated an agreement between the king and the company, began his career in a syndicate arranging contracts for [victualling the navy](/wiki/Victualling_Commissioners "Victualling Commissioners"), an interest he kept up for most of his life. He knew that [Samuel Pepys](/wiki/Samuel_Pepys "Samuel Pepys") and [John Evelyn](/wiki/John_Evelyn "John Evelyn") had amassed a substantial fortune from the [Levant](/wiki/Levant "Levant") and Indian trades.

He became a director and later, as governor of the East India Company in 1672, he arranged a contract which included a loan of £20,000 and £30,000 worth of [saltpetre](/wiki/Potassium_nitrate "Potassium nitrate")—also known as potassium nitrate, a primary ingredient in [gunpowder](/wiki/Gunpowder "Gunpowder")—for the King "at the price it shall [sell by the candle](/wiki/Candle_auction "Candle auction")"—that is by auction—where bidding could continue as long as an inch-long candle remained alight.[[81]](#cite_note-85)

Outstanding debts were also agreed and the company permitted to export 250 tons of saltpetre. Again in 1673, Banks successfully negotiated another contract for 700 tons of saltpetre at £37,000 between the king and the company. So high was the demand from armed forces that the authorities sometimes turned a blind eye on the untaxed sales. One governor of the company was even reported as saying in 1864 that he would rather have the saltpetre made than the tax on salt.[[82]](#cite_note-86)

#### Colonial monopoly

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=East_India_Company&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Colonial monopoly")]

Further information: [Great Britain in the Seven Years' War](/wiki/Great_Britain_in_the_Seven_Years%27_War "Great Britain in the Seven Years' War")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ed/The_East_offering_its_riches_to_Britannia_-_Roma_Spiridone%2C_1778_-_BL_Foster_245.jpg/330px-The_East_offering_its_riches_to_Britannia_-_Roma_Spiridone%2C_1778_-_BL_Foster_245.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_East_offering_its_riches_to_Britannia_-_Roma_Spiridone,_1778_-_BL_Foster_245.jpg)

*[The East Offering its Riches to Britannia](/wiki/The_East_Offering_its_Riches_to_Britannia "The East Offering its Riches to Britannia")* - Roma Spiridone, 1778 - BL Foster 245

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9c/E_India_House.jpg/330px-E_India_House.jpg)](/wiki/File:E_India_House.jpg)

An engraving of [East India House](/wiki/East_India_House "East India House"), in [Leadenhall Street](/wiki/Leadenhall_Street "Leadenhall Street") in the [City of London](/wiki/City_of_London "City of London") (1766)

The Seven Years' War (1756–1763) resulted in the defeat of the French forces, limited French imperial ambitions, and stunted the influence of the Industrial Revolution in French territories. [Robert Clive](/wiki/Robert_Clive "Robert Clive"), the Governor-General, led the company to a victory against [Joseph François Dupleix](/wiki/Joseph_Fran%C3%A7ois_Dupleix "Joseph François Dupleix"), the commander of the French forces in India, and recaptured Fort St George from the French. The company took this respite to seize [Manila](/wiki/Battle_of_Manila_(1762) "Battle of Manila (1762)") in 1762.[[83]](#cite_note-87)[*[better source needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability#Questionable_sources "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*]

By the [Treaty of Paris](/wiki/Treaty_of_Paris_(1763) "Treaty of Paris (1763)"), France regained the five establishments captured by the British during the war ([Pondichéry](/wiki/Pondicherry_district "Pondicherry district"), [Mahe](/wiki/Mah%C3%A9,_India "Mahé, India"), [Karaikal](/wiki/Karaikal "Karaikal"), [Yanam](/wiki/Yanam,_French_India "Yanam, French India") and [Chandernagar](/wiki/Chandernagar "Chandernagar")) but was prevented from erecting fortifications and keeping troops in Bengal (art. XI). Elsewhere in India, the French were to remain a military threat, particularly during the War of American Independence, and up to the capture of Pondichéry in 1793 at the outset of the French Revolutionary Wars without any military presence. Although these small outposts remained French possessions for the next two hundred years, French ambitions on Indian territories were effectively laid to rest, thus eliminating a major source of economic competition for the company.

In May 1772 the EIC stock price rose significantly. In June [Alexander Fordyce](/wiki/Alexander_Fordyce "Alexander Fordyce") lost £300,000 [shorting](/wiki/Short_(finance) "Short (finance)") EIC stock, leaving his partners liable for an estimated £243,000 in debts.[[84]](#cite_note-88) As this information became public, 20–30 banks across Europe collapsed during the [British credit crisis of 1772–1773](/wiki/British_credit_crisis_of_1772%E2%80%931773 "British credit crisis of 1772–1773").[[85]](#cite_note-89)[[86]](#cite_note-90) In India alone, the company had bill debts of £1.2 million. It seems that EIC directors [James Cockburn](/wiki/Sir_James_Cockburn,_8th_Baronet "Sir James Cockburn, 8th Baronet") and [George Colebrooke](/wiki/George_Colebrooke "George Colebrooke") were "[bulling](/wiki/Bull_(stock_market_speculator) "Bull (stock market speculator)")" the Amsterdam market during 1772.[[87]](#cite_note-91) The root of this crisis in relation to the East India Company came from the prediction by [Isaac de Pinto](/wiki/Isaac_de_Pinto "Isaac de Pinto") that "peace conditions plus an abundance of money would push East Indian shares to exorbitant heights".[[88]](#cite_note-sro.sussex.ac.uk-92)

> In September the company took out a loan from the Bank of England, to be repaid from the sale of goods later that month. But with buyers scarce, most of the sale had to be postponed, and when the loan fell due, the company's coffers were empty. On October 29 the bank refused to renew the loan. That decision set in motion a chain of events that made the American Revolution inevitable. The East India Company had eighteen million pounds of tea sitting in British warehouses. A huge amount of tea as assets which were lying unsold. Selling it in a hurry would do wonders for its finances.[[89]](#cite_note-93)

On 14 January 1773 the directors of the EIC asked for a government loan and unlimited access to the tea market in the American colonies, both of which were granted.[[90]](#cite_note-94) In August 1773 the [Bank of England](/wiki/Bank_of_England "Bank of England") assisted the EIC with a loan.[[91]](#cite_note-95)

The East India Company had also been granted competitive advantages over colonial American tea importers to sell tea from its colonies in Asia in American colonies. This led to the [Boston Tea Party](/wiki/Boston_Tea_Party "Boston Tea Party") of 1773 in which protesters boarded British ships and threw the tea overboard. When protesters successfully prevented the unloading of tea in three other colonies and in Boston, Governor [Thomas Hutchinson](/wiki/Thomas_Hutchinson_(governor) "Thomas Hutchinson (governor)") of the [Province of Massachusetts Bay](/wiki/Province_of_Massachusetts_Bay "Province of Massachusetts Bay") refused to allow the tea to be returned to Britain. This was one of the incidents which led to the [American Revolution](/wiki/American_Revolution "American Revolution") and independence of the American colonies.[[92]](#cite_note-96)

The company's trade monopoly with India was abolished in the [Charter Act 1813](/wiki/Charter_Act_1813 "Charter Act 1813"). The monopoly with China was ended in [1833](/wiki/Saint_Helena_Act_1833 "Saint Helena Act 1833"), ending the trading activities of the company and rendering its activities purely administrative.

### Disestablishment

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=East_India_Company&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Disestablishment")]

In the aftermath of the [Indian Rebellion of 1857](/wiki/Indian_Rebellion_of_1857 "Indian Rebellion of 1857") and under the provisions of the [Government of India Act 1858](/wiki/Government_of_India_Act_1858 "Government of India Act 1858"), the British Government nationalised the company. The British government took over its Indian possessions, its administrative powers and machinery, and its [armed forces](/wiki/Presidency_armies "Presidency armies").[[10]](#cite_note-Conquests-11)

The company had already divested itself of its commercial trading assets in India in favour of the UK government in 1833, with the latter assuming the debts and obligations of the company, which were to be serviced and paid from tax revenue raised in India. In return, the shareholders voted to accept an annual dividend of 10.5%, guaranteed for forty years, likewise to be funded from India, with a final pay-off to redeem outstanding shares. The debt obligations continued beyond dissolution and were only extinguished by the UK government during the Second World War.[[93]](#cite_note-97)

The company remained in existence in vestigial form, continuing to manage the tea trade on behalf of the British Government (and the supply of [Saint Helena](/wiki/Saint_Helena "Saint Helena")) until the [East India Stock Dividend Redemption Act 1873](/wiki/East_India_Stock_Dividend_Redemption_Act_1873 "East India Stock Dividend Redemption Act 1873") came into effect, on 1 January 1874. This act provided for the formal dissolution of the company on 1 June 1874, after a final dividend payment and the commutation or redemption of its stock.[[94]](#cite_note-98) *[The Times](/wiki/The_Times "The Times")* commented on 8 April 1873:[[4]](#cite_note-Times-5)

> It accomplished a work such as in the whole history of the human race no other trading Company ever attempted, and such as none, surely, is likely to attempt in the years to come.

## Establishments in Britain

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=East_India_Company&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Establishments in Britain")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/40/East_India_House_by_Thomas_Malton_the_Younger.jpg/250px-East_India_House_by_Thomas_Malton_the_Younger.jpg)](/wiki/File:East_India_House_by_Thomas_Malton_the_Younger.jpg)

The expanded [East India House](/wiki/East_India_House "East India House"), London, painted by [Thomas Malton](/wiki/Thomas_Malton "Thomas Malton"), c. 1800

The company's headquarters in London, from which much of India was governed, was [East India House](/wiki/East_India_House "East India House") in [Leadenhall Street](/wiki/Leadenhall_Street "Leadenhall Street"). After occupying premises in [Philpot Lane](/wiki/Philpot_Lane "Philpot Lane") from 1600 to 1621; in [Crosby House](/wiki/Crosby_Hall,_London "Crosby Hall, London"), [Bishopsgate](/wiki/Bishopsgate "Bishopsgate") from 1621 to 1638; and in Leadenhall Street from 1638 to 1648, the company moved into Craven House, an Elizabethan mansion in Leadenhall Street. The building had become known as East India House by 1661. It was completely rebuilt and enlarged in 1726–1729 and further significantly remodelled and expanded in 1796–1800. It was finally vacated in 1860 and demolished in 1861–1862.[[95]](#cite_note-99) The site is now occupied by the [Lloyd's building](/wiki/Lloyd%27s_building "Lloyd's building").[[96]](#cite_note-100)

In 1607, the company decided to build its own ships and leased a yard on the [River Thames](/wiki/River_Thames "River Thames") at [Deptford](/wiki/Deptford "Deptford"). By 1614, the yard having become too small, an alternative site was acquired at [Blackwall](/wiki/Blackwall_Yard "Blackwall Yard"): the new yard was fully operational by 1617. It was sold in 1656, although for some years East India Company ships continued to be built and repaired there under the new owners.[[97]](#cite_note-:0-101)

In 1803 an act of Parliament, promoted by the East India Company, established the East India Dock Company, with the aim of establishing a new set of docks (the [East India Docks](/wiki/East_India_Docks "East India Docks")) primarily for the use of ships trading with India. The existing Brunswick Dock, part of the Blackwall Yard site, became the Export Dock; while a new Import Dock was built to the north. In 1838 the East India Dock Company merged with the [West India Dock Company](/wiki/West_India_Docks "West India Docks"). The docks were taken over by the [Port of London Authority](/wiki/Port_of_London_Authority "Port of London Authority") in 1909 and closed in 1967.[[97]](#cite_note-:0-101)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/31/Addiscombe_Seminary_photo_c.1859.jpg/250px-Addiscombe_Seminary_photo_c.1859.jpg)](/wiki/File:Addiscombe_Seminary_photo_c.1859.jpg)

[Addiscombe Seminary](/wiki/Addiscombe_Military_Seminary "Addiscombe Military Seminary"), photographed in c. 1859, with cadets in the foreground

The [East India College](/wiki/East_India_Company_College "East India Company College") was founded in 1806 as a training establishment for "writers" (i.e. clerks) in the company's service. It was initially located in [Hertford Castle](/wiki/Hertford_Castle "Hertford Castle"), but moved in 1809 to purpose-built premises at [Hertford Heath](/wiki/Hertford_Heath "Hertford Heath"), Hertfordshire. In 1858 the college closed; but in 1862 the buildings reopened as a [public school](/wiki/Public_school_(United_Kingdom) "Public school (United Kingdom)"), now [Haileybury and Imperial Service College](/wiki/Haileybury_and_Imperial_Service_College "Haileybury and Imperial Service College").[[98]](#cite_note-102)[[99]](#cite_note-Farrington_1976-103)

The [East India Company Military Seminary](/wiki/Addiscombe_Military_Seminary "Addiscombe Military Seminary") was founded in 1809 at [Addiscombe](/wiki/Addiscombe "Addiscombe"), near [Croydon](/wiki/Croydon "Croydon"), Surrey, to train young officers for service in the company's armies in India. It was based in Addiscombe Place, an early 18th-century mansion. The government took it over in 1858 and renamed it the Royal Indian Military College. In 1861 it was closed, and the site was subsequently redeveloped.[[100]](#cite_note-104)[[99]](#cite_note-Farrington_1976-103): 111–123

In 1818, the company entered into an agreement by which those of its servants who were certified insane in India might be cared for at Pembroke House, [Hackney](/wiki/Hackney_(parish) "Hackney (parish)"), London, a private [lunatic asylum](/wiki/History_of_psychiatric_institutions "History of psychiatric institutions") run by Dr George Rees until 1838, and thereafter by Dr William Williams. The arrangement outlasted the company itself, continuing until 1870, when the India Office opened its own asylum, the [Royal India Asylum](/wiki/Hanwell#Healthcare "Hanwell"), at [Hanwell](/wiki/Hanwell "Hanwell"), Middlesex.[[99]](#cite_note-Farrington_1976-103): 125–132[[101]](#cite_note-105)

The [East India Club](/wiki/East_India_Club "East India Club") in London was formed in 1849 for officers of the company. The Club still exists today as a private [gentlemen's club](/wiki/Gentlemen%27s_club "Gentlemen's club") with its club house situated at 16 [St James's Square](/wiki/St_James%27s_Square "St James's Square"), London.[[102]](#cite_note-106)[[103]](#cite_note-107)

The English East India Company flag changed over time, with a [canton](/wiki/Canton_(flag) "Canton (flag)") based on the flag of the contemporary Kingdom, and a field of 9-to-13 alternating red and white stripes.

From 1600, the canton consisted of a [St George's Cross](/wiki/St_George%27s_Cross "St George's Cross") representing the [Kingdom of England](/wiki/Kingdom_of_England "Kingdom of England"). With the [Acts of Union 1707](/wiki/Acts_of_Union_1707 "Acts of Union 1707"), the canton was changed to the new [Union Flag](/wiki/Evolution_of_UK_Flag "Evolution of UK Flag")—consisting of an English St George's Cross combined with a Scottish [St Andrew's cross](/wiki/Flag_of_Scotland "Flag of Scotland")—representing the [Kingdom of Great Britain](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Great_Britain "Kingdom of Great Britain"). After the [Acts of Union 1800](/wiki/Acts_of_Union_1800 "Acts of Union 1800") that joined Ireland with Great Britain to form the [United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland](/wiki/United_Kingdom_of_Great_Britain_and_Ireland "United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland"), the canton of the East India Company flag was altered accordingly to include a [Saint Patrick's Saltire](/wiki/Saint_Patrick%27s_Saltire "Saint Patrick's Saltire").

There has been much debate about the number and order of stripes in the field of the flag. Historical documents and paintings show variations from 9-to-13 stripes, with some images showing the top stripe red and others showing it white.

At the time of the American Revolution the East India Company flag was nearly identical to the [Continental Union Flag](/wiki/Continental_Union_Flag "Continental Union Flag"). Historian [Charles Fawcett](/wiki/Charles_Fawcett_(historian) "Charles Fawcett (historian)") argued that the East India Company Flag inspired the [Stars and Stripes of America](/wiki/Flag_of_the_United_States "Flag of the United States").[[104]](#cite_note-108)

### Coat of arms

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=East_India_Company&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Coat of arms")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/58/Coat_of_Arms_of_East_India_Company_%281600-1709%29.svg/250px-Coat_of_Arms_of_East_India_Company_%281600-1709%29.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Coat_of_Arms_of_East_India_Company_(1600-1709).svg)

The original coat of arms of the East India Company (1600)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/23/Coat_of_arms_of_the_East_India_Company.svg/250px-Coat_of_arms_of_the_East_India_Company.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Coat_of_arms_of_the_East_India_Company.svg)

The later coat of arms of the East India Company (1698)

The East India Company's original [coat of arms](/wiki/Coat_of_arms "Coat of arms") was granted in 1600. The [blazon](/wiki/Blazon "Blazon") of the arms is as follows:[[105]](#cite_note-heraldry-109)

> Arms: *Azure, three ships with three masts, rigged and under full sail, the sails, pennants and ensigns Argent, each charged with a cross Gules; on a chief of the second a pale quarterly Azure and Gules, on the 1st and 4th a fleur-de-lis or, on the 2nd and 3rd a leopard or, between two roses Gules seeded Or barbed Vert.*
>
> [Crest](/wiki/Crest_(heraldry) "Crest (heraldry)"): *A sphere without a frame, bounded with the Zodiac in bend Or, between two pennants flottant Argent, each charged with a cross Gules, over the sphere the words *Deus indicat** ([Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin"): God Indicates).
>
> [Supporters](/wiki/Supporter "Supporter"): *Two sea lions Or, the tails proper.*
>
> Motto: **Deo ducente nil nocet** (Latin: Where God Leads, Nothing Harms)

The East India Company's later arms, granted in 1698, were:[[105]](#cite_note-heraldry-109)

> Arms: *Argent a cross Gules; in the dexter chief quarter an escutcheon of the [arms of France](/wiki/Coat_of_arms_of_France "Coat of arms of France") and [England](/wiki/Coat_of_arms_of_England "Coat of arms of England") quarterly, the shield ornamentally and regally crowned Or.*
>
> Crest: *A lion rampant guardant Or holding between the forepaws a regal crown proper.*  
> Supporters: *Two lions rampant guardant Or, each supporting a banner erect Argent, charged with a cross Gules.*
>
> Motto: *Auspicio regis et senatus angliæ* (Latin: Under the auspices of the King and the Parliament of England).

### Merchant mark

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=East_India_Company&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Merchant mark")]

* [![HEIC Merchant's mark on a Blue Scinde Dawk postage stamp (1852)](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b2/Symbols_on_a_Blue_Scinde_Dawk.jpg/120px-Symbols_on_a_Blue_Scinde_Dawk.jpg)](/wiki/File:Symbols_on_a_Blue_Scinde_Dawk.jpg "HEIC Merchant's mark on a Blue Scinde Dawk postage stamp (1852)")

  HEIC [Merchant's mark](/wiki/Merchant%27s_mark "Merchant's mark") on a Blue [Scinde Dawk](/wiki/Scinde_Dawk "Scinde Dawk") postage stamp (1852)
* [![1 Pice (1⁄64 Rupee) copper coin of the Bombay Presidency with bale mark (1821)](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1f/1_Pice_copper_coin_of_the_Bombay_Presidency.jpg/120px-1_Pice_copper_coin_of_the_Bombay_Presidency.jpg)](/wiki/File:1_Pice_copper_coin_of_the_Bombay_Presidency.jpg "1 Pice (1⁄64 Rupee) copper coin of the Bombay Presidency with bale mark (1821)")

  1 Pice (1⁄64 Rupee) copper coin of the [Bombay Presidency](/wiki/Bombay_Presidency "Bombay Presidency") with bale mark (1821)

When the East India Company was chartered in 1600, it was still customary for individual merchants or members of companies such as the [Company of Merchant Adventurers](/wiki/Company_of_Merchant_Adventurers_of_London "Company of Merchant Adventurers of London") to have a distinguishing [merchant's mark](/wiki/Merchant%27s_mark "Merchant's mark") which often included the mystical [Sign of Four](/wiki/Merchant%27s_mark#The_East_India_Company's_Merchant's_Mark "Merchant's mark") and served as a trademark. The East India Company's merchant mark consisted of a Sign of Four atop a heart within which was a [saltire](/wiki/Saltire "Saltire") between the lower arms of which were the initials "EIC". This mark was a central motif of the East India Company's coinage[[106]](#cite_note-110) and forms the central emblem displayed on the [Scinde Dawk](/wiki/Scinde_Dawk "Scinde Dawk") postage stamps.[[107]](#cite_note-111)

## Ships

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=East_India_Company&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Ships")]

See also: [East Indiaman](/wiki/East_Indiaman "East Indiaman") and [List of ports of call of the British East India Company](/wiki/List_of_ports_of_call_of_the_British_East_India_Company "List of ports of call of the British East India Company")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/43/Ships_in_Bombay_Harbour%2C_1731.jpg/500px-Ships_in_Bombay_Harbour%2C_1731.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ships_in_Bombay_Harbour,_1731.jpg)

Ships in [Bombay Harbour](/wiki/Bombay_Harbour "Bombay Harbour"), c. 1731

Ships of the East India Company were called [East Indiamen](/wiki/East_Indiaman "East Indiaman") or simply "Indiamen".[[108]](#cite_note-112) Their names were sometimes prefixed with the initials "HCS", standing for "Honourable Company's Service"[[109]](#cite_note-113) or "Honourable Company's Ship",[[110]](#cite_note-114) such as [HCS *Vestal* (1809)](/wiki/HCS_Vestal_(1809) "HCS Vestal (1809)") and [HCS *Intrepid* (1780)](/wiki/HCS_Intrepid_(1780) "HCS Intrepid (1780)").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c1/Indiaman_Royal_George.jpg/500px-Indiaman_Royal_George.jpg)](/wiki/File:Indiaman_Royal_George.jpg)

[*Royal George*](/wiki/Royal_George_(East_Indiaman) "Royal George (East Indiaman)") was one of the five East Indiamen the Spanish fleet captured in 1780

During the [French Revolutionary](/wiki/French_Revolutionary_Wars "French Revolutionary Wars") and [Napoleonic Wars](/wiki/Napoleonic_Wars "Napoleonic Wars"), the East India Company arranged for [letters of marque](/wiki/Letter_of_marque "Letter of marque") for its vessels such as *Lord Nelson*. This was not so that they could carry cannon to fend off warships, privateers, and pirates on their voyages to India and China (that they could do without permission) but so that, should they have the opportunity to take a prize, they could do so without being guilty of piracy.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Similarly, *Earl of Mornington*, an East India Company [packet ship](/wiki/Packet_ship "Packet ship") of only six guns, also sailed under a letter of marque.

In addition, the company had its own navy, the [Bombay Marine](/wiki/History_of_the_Indian_Navy "History of the Indian Navy"), equipped with warships such as [*Grappler*](/wiki/HCS_Grappler_(1804) "HCS Grappler (1804)"). These vessels often accompanied vessels of the Royal Navy on expeditions, such as the [Invasion of Java](/wiki/Invasion_of_Java_(1811) "Invasion of Java (1811)").

At the [Battle of Pulo Aura](/wiki/Battle_of_Pulo_Aura "Battle of Pulo Aura"), which was probably the company's most notable naval victory, [Nathaniel Dance](/wiki/Nathaniel_Dance "Nathaniel Dance"), Commodore of a convoy of Indiamen and sailing aboard the [*Warley*](/wiki/Warley_(1796_ship) "Warley (1796 ship)"), led several Indiamen in a skirmish with a French squadron, driving them off. Some six years earlier, on 28 January 1797, five Indiamen, *Woodford*, under Captain Charles Lennox, *Taunton-Castle*, Captain Edward Studd, *Canton*, Captain Abel Vyvyan, *Boddam*, Captain George Palmer, and [*Ocean*](/wiki/Ocean_(1788_EIC_ship) "Ocean (1788 EIC ship)"), Captain John Christian Lochner, had encountered Admiral [de Sercey](/wiki/Pierre_C%C3%A9sar_Charles_de_Sercey "Pierre César Charles de Sercey") and his squadron of frigates. On this occasion the Indiamen succeeded in bluffing their way to safety, and without any shots even being fired. Lastly, on 15 June 1795, *General Goddard* played a large role in the capture of seven Dutch East Indiamen off [St Helena](/wiki/Saint_Helena "Saint Helena").

[East Indiamen](/wiki/East_Indiamen "East Indiamen") were large and strongly built, and when the [Royal Navy](/wiki/Royal_Navy "Royal Navy") was desperate for vessels to escort merchant convoys, it bought several of them to convert to warships. *Earl of Mornington* became HMS *Drake*. Other examples include:

* [HMS *Calcutta*](/wiki/HMS_Calcutta_(1795) "HMS Calcutta (1795)")
* [HMS *Glatton*](/wiki/HMS_Glatton_(1795) "HMS Glatton (1795)")
* [HMS *Hindostan*](/wiki/HMS_Hindostan_(1795) "HMS Hindostan (1795)") (1795)
* [HMS *Hindostan*](/wiki/HMS_Hindostan_(1804) "HMS Hindostan (1804)") (1804)
* [HMS *Malabar*](/wiki/HMS_Malabar_(1804) "HMS Malabar (1804)")
* [HMS *Buffalo*](/wiki/HMS_Buffalo_(1813) "HMS Buffalo (1813)")

Their design as merchant vessels meant that their performance in the warship role was underwhelming and the Navy converted them to transports.

The amount of trade and shipping between Britain and company ports in India was considerable. For instance, in 1844, 534 ships (247,087 tons) arrived in Britain, and 540 vessels (239,368 tons) left UK ports for British India.[[111]](#cite_note-115)

## Records

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=East_India_Company&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Records")]

Main article: [India Office Records](/wiki/India_Office_Records "India Office Records")

Unlike all other British Government records, the records from the East India Company (and its successor the [India Office](/wiki/India_Office "India Office")) are not in [The National Archives](/wiki/The_National_Archives_(United_Kingdom) "The National Archives (United Kingdom)") at [Kew](/wiki/Kew "Kew"), London, but are held by the [British Library](/wiki/British_Library "British Library") in London as part of the [Asia, Pacific and Africa Collections](/wiki/Asia,_Pacific_and_Africa_Collections,_British_Library "Asia, Pacific and Africa Collections, British Library"). The catalogue is searchable online in the *[Access to Archives](/wiki/Access_to_Archives "Access to Archives")* catalogues.[[112]](#cite_note-116) Many of the East India Company records are freely available online under an agreement that the [Families in British India Society](/wiki/Families_in_British_India_Society "Families in British India Society") has with the British Library. Published catalogues exist of East India Company ships' journals and logs, 1600–1834;[[113]](#cite_note-117) accompanying catalogues also cover the company's daughter institutions, including the East India Company College, Haileybury, and Addiscombe Military Seminary.[[99]](#cite_note-Farrington_1976-103)

*[The Asiatic Journal and Monthly Register for British India and its Dependencies](/wiki/The_Asiatic_Journal_and_Monthly_Register_for_British_India_and_its_Dependencies "The Asiatic Journal and Monthly Register for British India and its Dependencies")*, first issued in 1816, was sponsored by the East India Company, and includes much information relating to its work.
